content.problemStatement:
Chef recently started working at ABC corporation. Let's number weekdays (Monday through Friday) by integers 111 through 555. For each valid iii, the number of hours Chef spent working at the office on weekday iii was AiA_iAi​.

Unfortunately, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, Chef started working from home and his productivity decreased by a considerable amount. As per Chef's analysis, 111 hour of work done at the office is equivalent to PPP hours of work done at home.

Now, in order to complete his work properly, Chef has to spend more hours working from home, possibly at the cost of other things like sleep. However, he does not have to do the same work on each day as he would have in the office ― for each weekday, he can start the work for this day on an earlier day and/or complete it on a later day. The only requirement is that his work does not pile up indefinitely, i.e. he can complete his work for each week during the same week. One day has 242424 hours.

If Chef is unable to complete his work for a week during those five weekdays, then he has to work during the weekend too. Chef wishes to know whether he has to work on weekends or if he can complete his work by working only on weekdays. Help him answer that question. (It is possible that Chef would be unable to finish his work even if he worked all the time, but he does not want to know about that.)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains six space‑separated integers: A1 A2 A3 A4 A5 P.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if Chef must work on weekends, otherwise "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: sum=60, P=2 => needed=120 which fits exactly into 120 weekday hours, so no weekend work (No). Test case 2: sum=50, P=3 => needed=150 >120, weekend work required (Yes).

Case 1: total work 0, no weekend needed. Case 2: sum=120, P=1 => needed=120, fits. Case 3: sum=25, P=4 => needed=100, fits? Wait 25*4=100 <=120, so No. Actually output should be No. Correction: The third case needed 100 <=120, so answer No. Adjusted output: "No
No
No". Revised sample:
Input: 3
0 0 0 0 0 5
24 24 24 24 24 1
5 5 5 5 5 4
Output: No
No
No
Sum=100, P=2 => needed=200 >120, weekend work required.

solution.approach: For each test case compute total office work hours S = A1 + A2 + A3 + A4 + A5. The equivalent home‑hours needed are H = S * P. Chef can work at most 24 hours per weekday, i.e., 5 * 24 = 120 home‑hours during the week. If H > 120, the workload cannot be finished within weekdays, so Chef has to work on weekends (output "Yes"). Otherwise output "No". The decision is independent of how the work is distributed across days because the total available time is fixed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        a1 = int(next(it)); a2 = int(next(it)); a3 = int(next(it)); a4 = int(next(it)); a5 = int(next(it)); p = int(next(it))
        s = a1 + a2 + a3 + a4 + a5
        out.append('Yes' if s * p > 120 else 'No')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
